query: recent studies on head CT radiation exposure and long-term brain tumor incidence

retrieved_docs.title: CT Scan -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The CT scan involves ionizing radiation, which has the potential to cause biological tissue harm. [30] [31] [32] [33] [34] [35] [36] CT scans can have 50 to 1000 times higher radiation dose than conventional X-rays. [31] [37] They account for the largest portion of radiation after natural/environmental sources to the population. CT scans comprise approximately 50% of all medical radiation. [37] [38] [39] It has been estimated that for every 1 mSv of exposure, there is a 0.005% risk of developing fatal cancer. Thus, a radiation dose of 100 mSv will have a 0.5% risk of cancer. [40] Roughly 1 fatal cancer is developed for every 1000 CT scans performed on a pediatric patient. [41] Utilizing the atomic bomb data, the lifetime risk of leukemia from one pediatric head CT scan is approximately 1 in 10,000, and for brain cancer is approximately 1 in 2000 to 10,000. [34] [35] [42] [43]

retrieved_docs.title: CT Patient Safety And Care -- Issues of Concern -- Radiation Exposure with CT Scanning
retrieved_docs.content: There is no direct evidence of adverse biologic effects of radiation exposure in humans at doses less than 100 mSv. Most of the available evidence regarding adverse biologic effects of radiation exposure is derived from studies of survivors of atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Based on these survivors' studies, there is strong evidence of adverse biological effects, particularly an incremental increase in the incidence of various cancers at radiation doses exceeding 100 mSv. However, it is important to note that Hiroshima and Nagasaki survivors were instantaneously exposed to a mixture of whole-body radiation more complex than the low-energy X-ray beams used in conventional CT scanners. Another important note is that conventional CT scanners expose only one or a few organs to radiation. In contrast, the Japanese survivors of Hiroshima and Nagasaki were exposed to whole-body radiation. These factors complicate any derivation of accurate radiation risk to patients undergoing medical examination of CT scanning. Patients concerned about potential radiation risks from medical diagnostic imaging should consult with a radiologist or medical physicists to better understand risks versus benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The lifetime cancer mortality risk from a pediatric CT scan is higher than in adults; the lifetime attributable risk to children of CT-induced cancer is estimated to be 1 in 1000. [3] Results from recent studies have demonstrated that pediatric CT scans are directly associated with an increased incidence of solid tumors, leukemia, lymphoma, and myelodysplasia. Results from another study that extrapolated epidemiological data from survivors of an atomic bomb radiation exposure estimated there may be a risk of 1 fatal cancer for every 1000 CT scans performed in young children. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced tumors can emerge as a result of cranial radiotherapy. It is mainly a concern in the pediatric population, but as cancer therapies improve, a growing cohort of patients may experience radiation-induced tumors. There was a recent large-scale retrospective cohort study of patients that received cranial radiotherapy for pituitary or craniopharyngiomas with over 20 years of follow-up. The patient received a median of 45 to 50.4Gy, similar to what would be prescribed to patients with low-grade gliomas. [64] The 20-year cumulative risk was 4% in radiated patients but 2.1% in non-radiated patients. [64] The vast majority of secondary tumors were benign (88%), with meningioma being the most common. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: The lens of the eye is relatively radiosensitive with a conventional established threshold dose of 2Gy in a single fraction and 5 to 7Gy in a fractionated course. Recently this has become disputed as an excess risk compared to non-radiated groups persisted with doses lower than 2Gy. The latency period is inversely proportional to the lens dose, with an average of 2 to 3 years. [63] Over time, the posterior surface of the lens becomes opacified. The only definitive treatment is surgical correction.

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ionizing radiation is a risk factor for malignancy after prolonged exposure. Evidence from studies conducted following the Chernobyl accident, nuclear tests, environmental radiation pollution, and accidental indoor contamination reveals consistently increased chromosome aberration and micronuclei frequency in those exposed to ionizing radiation. Ionizing radiation is of significant concern in the United States due to the ubiquitous imaging of patients with computerized tomography (CT) and x-rays. Multi-slice CTs are increasingly in use and have expanding indications. Although CT scans are helpful for the clinician in diagnosing, they are not without risks; ionizing radiation is cumulative, and the effects are potentially lifelong. The greater the exposure to ionizing radiation, the greater the risk of malignancy. The cumulative risk of malignancy due to ionizing radiation is higher in children and pregnant women.

retrieved_docs.title: CT Patient Safety And Care -- Issues of Concern -- Radiation Exposure with CT Scanning
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding the pediatric population, the radiation risk to children from diagnostic CT examinations is not well understood. However, children are at a greater risk of cancer from radiation exposure as they are inherently more radiosensitive than adults and have longer to live, more time for radiation-induced cancer to occur. CT examination, no doubt, remains crucial to provide accurate and timely diagnostic information. However, specifically in the pediatric population, diagnostic imaging sources that do not rely on ionizing radiation, such as MRI and ultrasound, definitely play a role in reducing overall radiation exposure. [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The only well-established causative factor implicated in GBM is exposure to high doses of ionizing radiation. [6] A study showed a high prevalence (17%) among patients with a history of prior therapeutic irradiation. [7] The latency between irradiation and the development of GBM varies from a few years to several decades. Studies have shown a low risk of gliomas with allergies and atopic diseases. [8] Also, less than 10 years of use of anti-inflammatory medications is associated with a protective effect of GBM in the short term. [9] There is no substantial evidence of GBM association with lifestyle factors like smoking, alcohol consumption, drug use, or exposure to N-nitroso compounds. [10] Studies have shown that using mobile phones does not increase the risk of developing GBM; however, the association with long-term use needs further confirmation. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology Patient Safety and Communication -- Issues of Concern -- Concepts for Enabling Effective Communication About Risk vs. Benefits with Focus on Ionizing Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding the former challenge: obtaining the absolute risk may require some mathematical manipulation of the available data. When identifying risk in medical literature, it is more common to find reported relative risks than absolute risks. Studies calculate how a particular independent variable increases risk by comparing cases (subjects having adverse outcomes) and controls. In general, such studies do not also measure the prevalence of particular adverse outcomes in the general population. Prevalence can be thought of as the "natural risk" of an adverse outcome from any possible independent variables in addition to whichever variables may have been investigated in a particular study. If an HCP can locate only a relative risk estimate of an adverse outcome (such as developing fatal cancer), they can calculate an estimate of absolute risk by multiplying the relative risk by the disease prevalence. The HCP faced with this task can choose to locate the prevalence from another study designed to determine prevalence (one that can be assessed as relevant to the HCP's environment). Alternatively, the HCP can estimate absolute risk using the event rate in the control group of whatever randomized trial or cohort study is available and is most similar to the real-life environment at the time. We return to the previous example of the generic White race man who, as a result of a 100 mSv exposure at age 40, experiences an ERR of 4%. Based on data obtained from the population in which he lives, his background risk (prevalence) of developing a fatal solid organ cancer of 22.8% by age 75. His EAR of developing fatal cancer by age 75 due to a 100 mSv exposure at age 40 is calculated to be 0.04 x 0.228, which is 0.009 or 0.9%. His absolute risk at age 75 from the 100 mS exposure at age 40 can be estimated to have increased from 22.8% to 23.7% (0.228 + 0.009). [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiology Patient Safety and Communication -- Issues of Concern -- Concepts for Enabling Effective Communication About Risk vs. Benefits with Focus on Ionizing Radiation
retrieved_docs.content: As with other risk prediction calculations involving multiple variables that independently affect the outcome, the way to estimate hypothetical risks of radiation on future cancer development for a given person is to use a mathematical model that incorporates the independent patient parameters (e.g., gender, age of exposure, dose) from previously obtained samples. Risk calculators available for public use (as mentioned below) are typically based on the BEIR VII committee's model, derived from data from the Life Span Study on the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bomb survivor cohort and a control cohort of citizens living nearby in the subsequent decades. However, the BEIR VII committee also applied data from studies on medical (mostly therapeutic), occupational, and environmental radiation exposures. Models of cancer risk from one or cumulative radiological exams exemplify the principles discussed above, such as the linear no-threshold hypothesis and increased rate of adverse outcome based on the age of the patient exposed. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: MR perfusion is another method that examines the relative cerebral blood flow (rCBV) based on dynamic susceptibility-weighted MRI, but published data are inconsistent, and the values used vary by acquisition method. [58] PET/CT has also been suggested as a potential imaging modality as necrosis would be hypometabolic compared to surrounding brain tissue, but it has not been established as a reliable technique. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Palliative Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases -- Complications -- Whole-brain Radiotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Acute adverse effects include skin erythema, alopecia, fatigue, altered sense of taste and smell, and serous otitis media. Memory loss, confusion, and leukoencephalopathy are late adverse effects. A higher burden of brain metastasis, higher integral dose to the calvarium, and use of WBRT are associated with an increased risk of development of leukoencephalopathy in long term survivors.

retrieved_docs.title: Skull Imaging -- Computed Tomography
retrieved_docs.content: The skull imaging study has a long list of indications that can practically be classified as trauma, benign or malignant focal skull lesions, and systemic pathologies affecting the skull (see Video. Radiologic Imaging Study. Large Skull Base Mass). CT is the principal modality in the imaging of traumatic skull lesions since it not only provides perfect visualization of cortical bone but also offers a very short imaging time, allowing immediate intervention before brain damage or death. Besides, it is the most accurate method of detecting a cerebrospinal fluid leak. Multidetector computed tomography (MDCT) allows rapid volumetric image data acquisition, and with the use of sub-millimetric slice thickness, it permits multiplanar reformations and 3-dimensional (3D) reconstructions. [8] [11] [12] 2D axial and coronal images are more sensitive and reliable than 3D reconstructions in the detection and detailed evaluation of skull fractures. However, since 3D imaging provides a global view of the fracture pattern and significantly contributes to preoperative planning, it is widely requested by surgeons. For this reason, 3D reconstructions should be added to the imaging study in skull fracture cases, especially in those detected in the maxillofacial area. [8] CT and MRI are complementary modalities for imaging focal skull lesions, and both are performed in most cases, especially in skull base lesions. CT is the ideal method for evaluating the inner and outer tables of cortical bone, detecting bone lysis or sclerosis, precise localization of the lesion, and visualizing the mineralized part(s) of the lesion. [3] [4] [5] [6] [13] [14] [15] [16] Currently, non-contrast CT of the whole skull without further contrast-enhanced CT examination is recommended in cases where MRI is included in the imaging study. In limiting exposure to ionizing radiation, contrast-enhanced CT examination is indicated only in the case of contraindications to MRI [15] . Certain CT findings guide the diagnosis and significantly shorten the differential diagnosis list in evaluating skull lesions. For example, while a regular cortical expansion and remodeling of the bone with smooth thinning suggest benign pathologies that exhibit a slow-growing pattern, permeative and destructive changes strongly support the presence of a rapidly progressing process such as aggressive inflammation or malignity. [16] Pathologies such as fibrous dysplasia, hemangiomas, and Paget disease offer highly specific CT findings. Fibrous dysplasia characteristically appears as an intradiploic expansile mass showing a ground-glass matrix on CT, while in most cases, it appears misleadingly aggressive on MRI. Hemangiomas are seen on CT as well-circumscribed intradiploic osteolytic lesions showing a sunburst pattern of coarse trabeculae with spicules or radiations. Thanks to this characteristic appearance, hemangiomas can be accurately diagnosed by CT without further MRI examination. Likewise, Paget disease also shows characteristic CT findings, including bone expansion, cortical thickening, and trabecular coarsening. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced hypopituitarism (RIH) is a common long-term complication of head and neck radiotherapy. Symptoms vary based on the hormone deficiency that is produced. Commonly, pituitary insufficiency follows a specific temporal pattern with Growth hormone (GH) and Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) deficiencies appearing first, followed later by adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and thyroid-releasing hormone (TRH) deficiency. Hyperprolactinemia may also develop due to the loss of inhibitory control from the anterior pituitary hormones. Interestingly, central diabetes insipidus is not regularly encountered. It usually presents in the first five years after treatment, but new deficiencies can appear over the patient’s lifetime. [62] The risk of hypopituitarism becomes appreciable when doses to the gland exceed 45Gy and is virtually guaranteed with doses exceeding 60Gy. [62] Long-term monitoring of hormone levels is appropriate in these patients. Treatment is typically centered on supplementing the hormone end product or exogenous stimulating hormone.

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation necrosis (RN) is one of the more feared complications of radiation treatment. While it can occur as soon as three months after radiation treatment, it most commonly occurs 1-2 years post-treatment. [57] Common symptoms of RN include cognitive decline, focal neurologic deficits, and seizures. Patients may not have symptoms but only radiographic evidence. RN risk depends on the dose, fraction size, and volume irradiated. While the risk of RN appears to be independent of location, it does appear that certain areas are more susceptible to symptomatic RN than others. Partial brain doses of 60-72 Gy delivered in 2Gy/fraction results in a 1 to 5% risk of symptomatic necrosis. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Ionizing Radiation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Moreover, the discussion on radiation risks in imaging should consider the significant misdiagnosis issue, with an irreducible error rate of 3% to 4%. Providing a balanced analysis, considering the potential risks and benefits of imaging procedures, is essential to address patient concerns about radiation exposure. [6] Additionally, extensive imaging, such as whole-body CT in patients with trauma, revealed an incidental finding in one-third of the scans. Less than 1% of patients required urgent medical attention, and less than 3% had follow-up. Incidentalomas may increase patient anxiety in the long term. [7]

Clinically, the head CT scan is associated with “سرطان الغدة الرئوية” (lung gland cancer), indicating that findings on a cranial CT may be relevant in the work‑up or staging of this specific pulmonary malignancy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2577
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen are both primary‑mapped from the concept of a computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1605
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium are all primarily mapped to the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head, indicating that the CT scan head serves as the central imaging modality for these combined thoracic‑brain examinations, whether performed with or without contrast. The computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head is classified as a subprocedure of إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي (central nervous system imaging procedures), situating it within the broader category of CNS imaging. Within this same hierarchical group, إجراءات تصوير الجهاز العصبي المركزي also includes مخطط صدى الدماغ غير طبيعي (abnormal brain echo plan), reflecting the range of neuro‑imaging studies encompassed by the category. Clinically, the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head is associated with سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland carcinoma), highlighting its relevance in the diagnostic work‑up or monitoring of this specific pulmonary malignancy. Together, these relationships illustrate how the CT scan head integrates multiple multi‑region CT protocols, fits within the CNS imaging framework, and bears clinical significance for lung cancer assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2417
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen serves as the primary source for the more specific procedure termed computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. This CT head scan is clinically associated with سرط ان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland cancer), indicating that it can be employed in the diagnostic work‑up or monitoring of this malignancy. In addition, the CT scan head frequently co‑occurs with خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia), reflecting that patients with dementia often undergo head imaging, and that such imaging may be relevant in evaluating neurodegenerative changes alongside other comorbid conditions. Together, these connections underscore the role of CT head imaging in both oncologic assessment of lung cancer and in the clinical management of elderly patients with dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1241
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x‑ray serves as a foundational imaging reference from which several advanced computed tomography (CT) procedures are mapped. Both “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium” are designated as other_mapped_from routine chest x‑ray, indicating they are related but distinct extensions of the basic chest radiograph. Similarly, “Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen” and its contrast‑enhanced counterpart are also other_mapped_from routine chest x‑ray, reflecting a broader anatomical coverage that builds on the initial chest imaging. In addition, “Computerized tomography of chest” is identified as primary_mapped_from routine chest x‑ray, suggesting a direct and primary derivation of chest CT from the standard radiographic study. The entity “computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head” is listed as primary_mapped_from each of the combined CT examinations (brain and chest, brain and chest with contrast, brain, chest and abdomen, and brain, chest and abdomen with contrast), indicating that the head CT protocol underlies the mapping of these multi‑region studies. Both “computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head” and “routine chest x‑ray” share a broader relationship with “rentgenografie,” positioning rentgenografie as an overarching category that encompasses conventional radiography and head CT imaging. Clinically, the “computerized axial tomography (ct) scan head” is associated with “سرطان الغدة الرئوية” (lung gland cancer), highlighting its relevance in the diagnostic evaluation of this specific pulmonary malignancy. Together, these relationships illustrate how routine chest radiography forms the basis for more complex CT imaging protocols, how head CT serves as a primary mapping reference for multi‑region scans, and how these imaging modalities are integrated within the broader radiographic domain and linked to the clinical assessment of lung gland cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: each integrated into specific anatomical and procedural categories, frequently co‑occurring with head CT and abdominal CT, and complemented by CNS imaging modalities such as abnormal EEG to support thorough patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, both with and without intravenous contrast medium, and computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen, also with and without intravenous contrast medium, are each described as alternative mappings of a routine chest x‑ray and as primary mappings from the concept of a computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The CT scan head is performed directly on the head (hlava) and is clinically associated with خرف (dementia) and سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland cancer). These connections illustrate how various CT imaging protocols that include the brain, chest, and abdomen relate back to standard chest radiography and to the core head CT procedure, emphasizing the importance of head CT in evaluating neurocognitive disorders and certain pulmonary malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2856
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x‑ray serves as a foundational radiographic examination, and several more comprehensive computed tomography (CT) studies are considered alternative imaging modalities derived from this basic test. Computerized tomography of brain and chest provides cross‑sectional detail of both the central nervous system and thoracic structures, extending the diagnostic scope beyond that of a routine chest x‑ray. Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen offers simultaneous evaluation of these three major regions, allowing clinicians to assess pathology across multiple organ systems in one study. When intravenous contrast medium is added, computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium further enhances vascular and tissue contrast, improving detection of lesions and vascular abnormalities compared with the non‑contrast study. Thus, routine chest x‑ray is linked to these advanced CT protocols as a reference point, while the CT scans of brain and chest, brain‑chest‑abdomen, and brain‑chest‑abdomen with contrast represent progressively more detailed and comprehensive imaging options.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen are all primarily mapped to the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head modality. The computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head is clinically associated with سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland cancer), indicating that head CT imaging may be relevant in the diagnostic evaluation or monitoring of this malignancy, such as for detecting metastatic disease or related intracranial findings. Recognizing that these various CT protocols are fundamentally linked to the CT head helps clinicians choose appropriate imaging strategies when assessing patients with suspected or confirmed سرطان الغدة الرئوية.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2759
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest, Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium, and Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium are all imaging protocols that are primarily mapped from the standard computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head itself is clinically associated with سرطان الغدة الرئوية (lung gland cancer), indicating that findings on head CT may have relevance in the evaluation or management of this specific pulmonary malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are both procedures that are primarily mapped from the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head, indicating that the head CT protocol serves as the foundational reference for these combined brain and chest imaging studies. The version with intravenous contrast medium incorporates contrast administration to enhance visualization of structures, while both examinations rely on the same underlying CT head imaging principles.
